section: Introduction to the Argo CD Agent architecture
job_statement: When managing multiple OpenShift clusters with GitOps, I want to understand the Argo CD Agent architecture and its capabilities, so I can determine if it fits my multi-cluster deployment needs.
desired_outcomes: Minimize time to evaluate architecture options
Reduce risk of choosing incompatible architecture
Ensure architecture supports my use cases
notes: This is an architectural overview job - understanding before implementing

job_statement: When evaluating Argo CD Agent, I want to understand the hub-and-spoke model and key terminology, so I can communicate effectively with my team about the architecture.
parent_job: Understand the Argo CD Agent architecture and its capabilities
desired_outcomes: Ensure clear understanding of control plane vs workload cluster roles
Minimize confusion about agent terminology
Enable effective team communication
notes: Defines control plane cluster (hub) and workload cluster (spoke) concepts

section: Comparing Argo CD Agent with traditional Argo CD architecture
job_statement: When choosing between traditional Argo CD and Argo CD Agent, I want to compare their capabilities and tradeoffs, so I can select the best approach for my multi-cluster requirements.
prerequisites: Understand the Argo CD Agent architecture and its capabilities
desired_outcomes: Minimize risk of choosing wrong architecture
Ensure architecture meets security requirements
Ensure architecture scales to my cluster count
Reduce likelihood of needing to migrate later
notes: Covers single pane of glass, network connectivity, scalability, security, local reconciliation, single point of failure, complexity, and maturity

section: Synchronization mechanism between the control plane and workload clusters
job_statement: When operating Argo CD Agent, I want to understand how resources synchronize between control plane and workload clusters, so I can troubleshoot sync issues and ensure proper operation.
parent_job: Understand the Argo CD Agent architecture and its capabilities
prerequisites: Understand the Argo CD Agent architecture and its capabilities
desired_outcomes: Minimize time to diagnose sync issues
Ensure consistent application state across clusters
Reduce risk of drift between control plane and workload clusters
notes: Covers resource synchronization and unified observability capabilities

job_statement: When configuring Argo CD Agent, I want to choose between Managed and Autonomous modes, so I can align the architecture with my operational model and security requirements.
prerequisites: Understand the Argo CD Agent architecture and its capabilities
Compare traditional Argo CD with Argo CD Agent
desired_outcomes: Ensure mode aligns with security requirements
Minimize operational complexity
Reduce risk of control plane compromise
Ensure mode supports required deployment patterns
notes: Two modes: Managed (control plane defines specs) and Autonomous (workload clusters define specs). Can also use mixed mode.

job_statement: When using centralized application management, I want to deploy Argo CD Agent in Managed mode, so I can define and manage all applications from the control plane while improving security over traditional Argo CD.
parent_job: Choose between Managed and Autonomous modes
prerequisites: Understand the Argo CD Agent architecture and its capabilities
desired_outcomes: Enable centralized application management
Eliminate need for workload cluster credentials
Reduce attack surface compared to traditional Argo CD
Ensure changes propagate reliably to workload clusters
evidence: argocd-agent-architecture-overview-combined.adoc -> Lines 102-124: Managed mode section with advantages and limitations
notes: Advantages: familiar centralized experience, eliminates cluster credentials. Limitations: limited app-of-apps support, control plane is single point of failure

job_statement: When prioritizing GitOps principles and resilience, I want to deploy Argo CD Agent in Autonomous mode, so I can maintain application definitions in Git while gaining centralized observability without control plane as single point of failure.
parent_job: Choose between Managed and Autonomous modes
prerequisites: Understand the Argo CD Agent architecture and its capabilities
Have Git repository for application definitions
desired_outcomes: Ensure Git is single source of truth for applications
Remove control plane as single point of failure
Enable app-of-apps deployment patterns
Maintain centralized visibility across clusters
evidence: argocd-agent-architecture-overview-combined.adoc -> Lines 126-146: Autonomous mode section with advantages and limitations
notes: Advantages: Git as single source of truth, no single point of failure, supports app-of-apps. Limitations: cannot modify apps from control plane UI

job_statement: When securing multi-cluster Argo CD deployments, I want to implement mTLS certificate-based authentication between control plane and agents, so I can ensure secure communication and prevent MITM attacks.
prerequisites: Choose between Managed and Autonomous modes
desired_outcomes: Ensure secure communication between clusters
Enable certificate rotation and expiration policies
Reduce risk of credential exposure
Prevent Man-in-the-Middle attacks
notes: Uses mutual TLS (mTLS) certificates. Root CA signs both principal and agent certificates. User is responsible for generating and managing certificates.

job_statement: When managing applications from multiple workload clusters on the control plane, I want to configure namespace isolation using sourceNamespaces, so I can maintain strict separation between workload cluster applications while enabling centralized monitoring.
prerequisites: Choose between Managed and Autonomous modes
desired_outcomes: Ensure strict isolation between workload cluster applications
Enable control plane to discover applications from all clusters
Minimize risk of cross-cluster interference
Reduce complexity of multi-cluster application management
evidence: argocd-agent-architecture-overview-combined.adoc -> Lines 166-231: Application Management Across Namespaces section with example configuration
notes: Uses 'Applications in any namespace' feature. Each workload cluster gets dedicated namespace on control plane (e.g., argocd-w1, argocd-w2). Configure via sourceNamespaces field in ArgoCD CR.

job_statement: When planning to deploy Argo CD Agent, I want to understand current limitations and unsupported features, so I can avoid deployment failures and plan for workarounds or future feature availability.
parent_job: Understand the Argo CD Agent architecture and its capabilities
related_jobs: Compare traditional Argo CD with Argo CD Agent
desired_outcomes: Minimize risk of hitting unsupported use cases
Ensure requirements can be met with current feature set
Plan for future feature availability
Reduce likelihood of deployment failures
notes: Active development. Limitations: limited namespace support on workload clusters, partial ApplicationSet support, limited app-of-apps in managed mode, no pod logs/terminal from control plane, no principal agent HA, RBAC/multi-tenancy under development
